edges.description: Loss of repression at the FSHD locus permits aberrant DUX4 expression in differentiated skeletal muscle.
Severe early-onset FSHD with large 4q35 deletions can include sensorineural hearing loss through extra-muscular intermediates not represented in the skeletal-muscle pathograph.
Recurrent DUX4-mediated injury drives chronic muscle degeneration with incomplete repair and replacement by fat and fibrous tissue.
edges.target: Progressive Myofiber Loss and Fatty Replacement

edges.description: Early involvement of the muscles of facial expression contributes to incomplete eye closure, poor smile excursion, and facial asymmetry.
edges.source: Progressive Myofiber Loss and Fatty Replacement

edges.description: Periscapular weakness destabilizes the scapulothoracic joint and limits active shoulder elevation.
edges.source: Progressive Myofiber Loss and Fatty Replacement

edges.description: Progressive weakness extends to upper arm, trunk, pelvic-girdle, and distal lower-extremity muscles.
edges.source: Progressive Myofiber Loss and Fatty Replacement

edges.description: Chronic biomechanical strain and ongoing muscle pathology contribute to frequent shoulder and low-back pain.
edges.source: Progressive Myofiber Loss and Fatty Replacement
D4Z4 repeat contraction on permissive 4qA haplotype

nodes.description: FSHD develops when D4Z4 contraction or contraction-independent hypomethylation occurs on a permissive 4qA subtelomere, relaxing chromatin and enabling pathogenic DUX4 expression.

evidence.reference_title: Common epigenetic changes of D4Z4 in contraction-dependent and contraction-independent FSHD.
evidence.snippet: To develop FSHD, D4Z4 contraction needs to occur on a specific genetic background. Only contractions associated with the 4qA161 haplotype cause FSHD.
Recent studies demonstrate that contraction in the number of D4Z4 repeats results in chromatin relaxation and transcriptional de-repression of DUX4, a gene normally expressed only in the germline.

nodes.id: D4Z4 repeat contraction on permissive 4qA haplotype

evidence.reference_title: Specific sequence variations within the 4q35 region are associated with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Yet only repeat contractions of D4Z4 on chromosome 4qA cause FSHD; contractions on the other chromosomes are nonpathogenic.

nodes.description: Intermittent DUX4 expression in skeletal muscle activates an embryonic transcriptional program and promotes muscle fiber toxicity and death.

evidence.reference_title: p38α Regulates Expression of DUX4 in a Model of Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD) is caused by the loss of repression at the D4Z4 locus leading to aberrant double homeobox 4 (DUX4) expression in skeletal muscle. Activation of this early embryonic transcription factor results in the expression of its target genes causing muscle fiber death.

nodes.description: Weakness of the facial musculature is often an early and characteristic manifestation of FSHD.

evidence.reference_title: Specific sequence variations within the 4q35 region are associated with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Autosomal dominant facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD) is mainly characterized by progressive wasting and weakness of the facial, shoulder, and upper-arm muscles.

nodes.description: Weakness is typically asymmetric and descends from facial and shoulder muscles to the upper arms, trunk, distal lower extremities, and pelvic girdle.

evidence.reference_title: Chronic pain has a strong impact on quality of life in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: FSHD symptoms usually start around the second decade and are most commonly characterized by asymmetric weakness affecting the face, shoulder, and arms, followed by the distal lower extremities and pelvic girdle.

nodes.description: Chronic pain commonly affects the shoulders and lower back and is an important contributor to disability and reduced quality of life.

evidence.reference_title: Chronic pain has a strong impact on quality of life in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Of 398 patients, 88.6% reported pain at the time of study. The most frequent locations were shoulders and lower back.

nodes.description: The clinical pattern of FSHD reflects chronic degeneration of selected facial, scapular, humeral, truncal, and lower-extremity muscles, usually with marked asymmetry and variable progression.
nodes.id: Progressive Myofiber Loss and Fatty Replacement

evidence.reference_title: Chronic pain has a strong impact on quality of life in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: FSHD symptoms usually start around the second decade and are most commonly characterized by asymmetric weakness affecting the face, shoulder, and arms, followed by the distal lower extremities and pelvic girdle.

nodes.description: Weakness of scapular stabilizers produces winging and limits active shoulder abduction and flexion.

evidence.reference_title: Fixation of winged scapula in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Participants: Thirteen patients with bilateral winged scapula affected by facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.

nodes.description: Hearing impairment is an extra-muscular feature seen mainly in severe early-onset disease with very short 4q35 fragments.

evidence.reference_title: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy: hearing loss and other atypical features of patients with large 4q35 deletions.
evidence.snippet: Sensorineural hearing loss was found in four patients, who presented infantile-onset dystrophic phenotype.
